title: Order in the Church
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 7-7-24 -AM- Order in the Church - 1 Peter 5_1-4 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: God Is a God of Order
subpoints: Order is seen throughout creation — from atoms to galaxies
God has established orderly government in family, state, and church
1 Peter 5 assumes the church already understands elder government

outline.title: Christ Is the Head of the Church
subpoints: Christ secured his headship through incarnation, obedience, death, resurrection, and ascension
The church is the fullness of Christ
No pastor or session is the lord of the church — Christ alone is

outline.title: Christ Appointed Presbyterian Government
subpoints: Government by elders (Presbyterianism) is Christ's design for his church
Office bearers are chosen by the people with defined qualifications
Examples from Acts: Matthias (Acts 1), Deacons (Acts 6), Elders (Acts 14:23)
The terms bishop, elder, and pastor all refer to the same office
Each local church should have multiple elders
Teaching elders (pastors) labor in word and doctrine (1 Tim. 5:17)

outline.title: The Church Is an Organic Unity
subpoints: Local churches are not independent from one another
The Jerusalem Council (Acts 15) demonstrates broader church authority
Decisions by assembled elders were binding on local churches

outline.title: Practical Benefits of Presbyterian Government
subpoints: Safety: elders protect the flock from wolves
Wisdom: godly mature men provide counsel
Accountability: elders are accountable to God and to one another
Balance: power is distributed, not concentrated in one person

outline.title: The Congregation's Duty
subpoints: Members are called to obey and submit to their elders
Elders serve as under-shepherds of the Chief Shepherd, Christ
Christ alone holds final authority; elders who claim otherwise must be rejected

summary: Preached as an introductory sermon to 1 Peter 5:1-4, this message lays the theological groundwork for understanding elder-rule in the church. Pastor Tito Lyro begins by observing that God is a God of order — visible in creation and instituted in family, civil, and church government. The sermon's central thesis is that Christ, as the head and king of the church, appointed a Presbyterian form of government (government by elders) for his people. Drawing on passages from Acts, the Pastoral Epistles, and 1 Peter itself, the pastor demonstrates that the offices of elder, bishop, and pastor are identical, that multiple elders per congregation is the biblical norm, and that congregations are not fully independent but connected through broader assemblies. The sermon explains how officeholders are elected by the people but represent Christ rather than the congregation, and closes with a call for congregational submission to elders as an act of obedience to Christ, alongside a pastoral exhortation to those outside of Christ to come to him who is both head and savior of his people.
transcript:
## Introduction: God Is a God of Order

If you're able, you remain standing for the reading of God's Word. We're reading out of 1 Peter chapter 5 as we continue on our series on 1 Peter. When read verses 1 through 4 of 1 Peter chapter 5, this is the word of our Lord. We're taught in seminary to wait to pages not turn in anymore before you start reading. So 1 Peter 5 verse 1, The elders who are among you, I exhort, I whom a fellow elder in a witness of the sufferings of Christ, and also a partaker of the glory that will be revealed. Shepherd the flock of God which is among you, serving as overseers not by compulsion but willingly, not for dishonest gain but eagerly. Nor as being lords over those entrusted to you, but being examples to the flock. And when the chief shepherd appears, you will receive the crown of glory that does not fade away. This door of our Lord, let us pray. Father, we do pray that you spirit, would you use your word today to speak to our hearts, pray that hearts will be changed, you pray Father, that you would be glorified, give me the strength to other words that are helpful to your people. We pray in Jesus name. Amen. Please be seated.

A few years ago, I read a book about the theory of relativity as it relates to the space, time, continue. Perhaps not a book that you choose to read, but that's the kind of stuff I enjoy reading at times for fun. And now you're thinking, I'm never going to go to a party that he organizes. But in that book, physicists marveled at the complex level of organization in space. Everywhere they looked in the universe, they found organization things ordered. From the structure of the fathers to galaxies to the strong and weak nuclear forces in the nucleus of the atom, the physicists, the scientists find absolute order. Everything is ordered. Even black holes behave in an orderly fashion. And after looking at this highly organized universe, the experts conclude that it all must have happened randomly. Well, we know that it was God who fashion the universe, and that it is orderly as a reflection of God's nature because God is a God of order. In 1st Corinthians chapter 14 verses 33 and 40, the Apostle Paul says, for God is not the author of confusion, but of peace as in all the churches of the saints, that all things be done decently and in order.

And this is true not only of the physical universe, but also of the spiritual and social realms. They say order everywhere. God instituted in orderly family, where roles are clearly defined for husband's wise parents and children. God instituted a civil government as a minister of order, and God instituted the visual church to be the order that orderly governed institutes to be governed by the elder. And as we are going to see today, elders were appointed by God to rule his church according to his word in an orderly fashion. Elders then were given to the church as a gift from God for the equipping of the saints for the work of the ministry.

So today's sermon actually is the introduction. So the whole sermon is introduction to next week's sermon. And we're going to do things a little different that what we used to do in the youths, most Sundays, where we work our way through a passage, expose it and explain how each of our connections are, how the verses work together, and then applying. Today we're going to look at a topic. So we're going to be looking at a lot of different passages in preparation to next week's sermon.

## Christ Is the Head of the Church

But as you notice here in the passage that we read this morning, Peter assumes that the church is that he's writing to and we know from verse 1, chapter 1, verse 1, that he's writing to the church's empantus, Galatia, Capadosia, Asia, and Biscini, which is in northern, modern day, northern Turkey. We'll know what he's talking about. He doesn't explain what elders are, he doesn't explain what their function is. He just assumes out of the blue that they know he's talking about when in verse 5, he says, the elders among you, I exhort.

As we think of the church of Jesus Christ, the first thing we have to keep in mind is that Christ is the head. Christ is the head, he is the boss, he is the life source, the one who gives directions. He is the head of the church. He is the king of the church. He is the governor of the church, and he secured his headship by becoming a man, so he is in incarnation, by obeying God perfectly with the perfect obedience that you and I cannot obey in his life. By dying across a death that you and I are sinners deserve, by being raised from the dead in that knowledge as the one who is the Savior of humanity, by his ascension into heaven, and by his sitting at the right hand of the Father.

The apostle Paul says in Colossians chapter 1 verses 15 and 18, he is the image of the invisible God talking about Christ. The first born over all creation for by him all things are created that are in heaven and that are on earth, visible and invisible whether thrones or dominions or pissipalons or powers. All things are created through him and for him and he is before all things, and in him all things consist, and he is the head of the body, the church who is the beginning, the first born from the dead, that in all things he may have the preaminess, first place.

In Ephesians chapter 1, starting verse 15, Paul's praying for the church. Starting verse 20, which he, the Father, worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly places, far above all, principality and power and might and dominion and every name that is named, no only in this age, but also in which is to come and, he, the Father, put all things other, his feet, Christ's feet, and gave him to be the head over all things to the church, and he is his body, the fullness of him, the church, is the fullness of Christ, who fills all in all.

In Ephesians chapter 4, verse 11, the Apostle says, he himself gave some to be Apostles, some prophets, some evangelists, and some pastor teachers, for the equipping of the saints, for the work of ministry, for the edifying of the body of Christ, till we all come to the unity of the faith and of the knowledge of the son of God, to a perfect man, to the measure of the statue of the fullness of Christ. In chapter 5, verse 23, the Apostle says, for the husband is the head of the wife, as also Christ is the head of the church, and he's the savior of the body.

## Christ Appointed Presbyterian Government

So you never want to be part of a church in which men think that they are the ultimate boss. Because the church has only one head, only one boss, and that is the Lord Jesus Christ. If anyone claims to stand in the place of Christ, run, because no one can do that, and Christ will not share his glory with anyone.

And here, Christ, the Lord of the Church, established a Presbyterian form of government for his church, which just means government by elders. That's how he wants his church to look. And we see that throughout the Scriptures. In this government that is exercise by elders, we see that the office bearers in the church are chosen by the people.

In Acts chapter 1, when the disciples needed to replace Judas, they came up with two men, Matthias and Justus, and the people voted between the two. In Acts chapter 6, when the church had conflict over the distribution to Greek-speaking widows, they established the office of Deacon. The church found seven men filled with the spirit, and then it says that they chose them by a vote. In chapter 14 of Acts, verse 23, Paul and his friends had appointed elders in every church and prayed with fasting — the word means they appointed elders by the showing of hands of the people.

But it is not a representative government. Elders are not here to represent you. They are here to represent Christ. And different words are used to talk about this same job. Sometimes they are called bishops. Sometimes they are called elders. Sometimes they are called pastors. But they are all talking about the same job, the same office. The Bible uses them that way. In Acts 20, he calls for the elders from Ephesus, and when they come, says that the Holy Spirit has made them overseers — bishops — to shepherd — pastor — the church of God.

The Bible says Christ wants more than one elder per local church. Acts 14:23 says they appointed elders plural in every church. In 1 Timothy 5:17-18, Paul says let the elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in the word and doctrine — the teaching elders, whom we today call pastors.

## The Church Is an Organic Unity

The Bible also says that each local church is not supposed to be independent from each other. The church was built to be an organic unity. In Acts 15, the Jerusalem Council, all the elders of the churches gathered together, made a decision that was binding upon all the churches they represented. The gathering of elders and the decisions by appeal affected and had to be followed by the local church.

## Practical Benefits of Presbyterian Government

God means for the people in the church to obey the elders that Christ has placed over them. Hebrews 13:17 says, obey those who rule over you and be submissive, for they watch out for your souls as those who must give account.

Why did God choose to do things this way? There are practical benefits. First, safety in numbers — elders protect the sheep from wolves in sheep's clothing and from destructive doctrines (Hebrews 13:17; Acts 20:28-30). Second, wisdom — godly mature men chosen for their character are available for counsel. Third, accountability — questions are called to exhort one another; if elders fail, broader church structures can correct them. Fourth, balance — the power does not reside in any one person.

## Closing Appeal

Our God is good and He does good. He did not leave us to figure out how we should be governed as a church. He gave us a system of doctrine, a system of government, directing us for the good of the sheep and for his glory. And the greatest thing you have heard today is Christ is our head. And He is our head because He is also our Savior. If you are not believing in Jesus Christ today, you are outside of the protection of Christ, outside of the saving work of Christ. He doesn't call you to reform your life. He doesn't call you to do anything but know that you are a sinner — and come to Him completely empty. God does not want perfect people. He already has His perfect son. He wants people who know they are broken, sinful failures. And to them He extends His hand and brings them into the fold and puts Christ over them as their head, leading them to all eternity.

Let us pray together. Father, thank you that you are a God who cares for your church. And you've given us the Lord Jesus Christ as our head. Father, help us to be a blessing to each other and to the world as we follow Christ, in His name. Amen.
